Section 26.13
AIDS
Copyright © 2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. No reproduction or distribution without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education.

Retroviruses, HIV, and AIDS
Human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) is a retrovirus —its genetic material is RNA, which is reverse-transcribed into DNA
Host cells in the immune system transcribe and translate the DNA, reproducing the virus
Treatment can center on disrupting reverse transcription using “faux-nucleotides”:
And/or, protease inhibitors can be used to disrupt the breakdown of long polypeptide chains into functional viral proteins

Section 26.14
DNA SEQUENCING

Challenges of DNA Sequencing
NA polymers are very large, even relative to proteins
There are only four nucleotides, which makes it difficult to distinguish between them for sequencing
There are no NA analogues of proteases that cleave at specific sequences
Even if there were, the sequences would be too small to be useful

Restriction Enzymes
Very long continuous sequencing is impractical, so NA chains must be cleaved to simplify the problem
Bacterial restriction enzymes cut DNA at specific sequences of nucleotides
After treatment with a set of restriction enzymes, fragments can be sequenced individually and “stitched” back together

DNA Sequencing
DNA to be sequenced is placed under replicating conditions in a solution of deoxyribonucleotide triphosphates: dGTP, dCTP, dATP, and dTTP
Nucleotide triphosphates missing the 3’ –OH group are also included!

DNA Sequencing
Elongation halts when one of these “ddNTPs” is incorporated; a fragment of complementary single-stranded DNA results
Electrophoresis separates the various fragments by size
Spectroscopy is used to characterize the ddNTP incorporated into each fragment

Section 26.15
THE HUMAN GENOME PROJECT

The Human Genome
In 2003, the finalized sequence of the 3 billion base pairs in the human genome were first published
The human genome has only about 25,000 genes—what are all those base pairs for?
The field of genomics addresses the sequences and functions of genes
Next generation sequencing (NGS):  various methods that sequence nucleic acid polymers in a massively parallel manner—millions and even billions of strands are sequenced simultaneously

Section 26.16
DNA PROFILING AND THE POLYMERASE CHAIN REACTION

DNA Profiling
The small amount of unique human DNA in noncoding regions can be used to identify individuals ( DNA profiling )
Used, for example, in criminal investigations
Problem: a very small portion of DNA is unique; the small section of DNA of interest must be replicated many times
This replication is done synthetically—with the help of an enzyme—via the polymerase chain reaction (PCR)

Overview of PCR
Single-stranded primers are prepared that are complementary to the sequences that flank the sequence of interest
Heating causes the double-stranded DNA to separate
In the annealing step, primers bind to the flanking sequences
In the synthesis step, the enzyme DNA polymerase and a “soup” of nucleotides cause extension of the primers
Steps 2 – 4 are repeated many times, eventually forming huge amounts of primer-target-primer DNA
The Sample DNA
Heating to 95 °C
Annealing at 65 °C
Synthesis (Chain Extension)
Denaturation + Priming, Cycle 2

Synthesis, Cycle 2
After one more round of denaturation, annealing, and synthesis, primer-target-primer DNA appears.

Target DNA Distribution in PCR
TABLE 26.4 Distribution of DNAs with Increasing Number of PCR Cycles
Cycle number | Total number of DNAs* | Number of DNAs containing only the target region
0 (start) | 1 | 0
1 | 2 | 0
2 | 4 | 0
3 | 8 | 2
4 | 16 | 8
5 | 32 | 22
10 | 1,024 | 1,004
20 | 1,048,566 | 1,048,526
30 | 1,073,741,824 | 1,073,741,764

Section 26.17
RECOMBINANT DNA TECHNOLOGY

Recombinant DNA Technology
Plasmids are circular DNA separate from chromosomal DNA in bacteria
Plasmids can be “snipped” using restriction enzymes and new sequences (such as sequences for human proteins!) inserted
As the bacteria live and replicate, they produce large amounts of the desired protein
When the time is right, the protein is harvested and purified
Recombinant DNA Technology-1
Recombinant DNA Technology-2
